tier: H4 / H-d: HFS+ general fragmenting allocator + multi-leaf extents-overflow B-tree + recursive extents-file guard
host: Windows build of Apple diskdev_cmds 540.1 fsck_hfs (the Linux/portable source Apple ships, compiled with gcc 16 in WSL Arch). newfs_hfs (same source) formatted the volume; the S.A.K. HFS+ writer CLI (sak_hfs_writer_cli.exe) produced the fragmentation; fsck_hfs validated. macOS-kernel mount + native-Apple fsck_hfs read-back is the follow-on confirmation (mac_kernel_mount_cert.sh).
change: Implemented the H-d driver core: (1) a streaming multi-leaf extents-overflow B-tree mutation engine mirroring the certified catalog model (load whole tree -> apply per-leaf insert/remove edits -> split overfull / merge underfull leaves -> rebuild index levels bottom-up -> compose header), replacing the bounded 'empty or single-leaf, one root-leaf split' engine; (2) the existing first-fit fragmenting allocation-bitmap allocator now threads an arbitrary number of fragments through the inline 8 extents plus an unbounded set of extents-overflow records across multiple leaves; (3) the extents-overflow file's own fork is held to the eight volume-header extents (TN1150: the extents file has no overflow extents - confirmed in SVerify1.c:3835 / SExtents.c:922), failing closed with a clear message at a ninth.
scenario: newfs_hfs -b 512 -n e=1024 formats a 16 MiB HFS+ volume (512-byte allocation blocks, 1024-byte extents B-tree node = 12 overflow records per leaf). Two files are created, then grown one 512-byte block at a time for 130 interleaved rounds; the first-fit allocator interleaves their blocks so each file ends with ~131 single-block extents. Each grow re-mutates the extents-overflow tree (remove the file's old records, re-insert the recomputed set), so the multi-leaf engine is exercised ~260 times. The result is a DEPTH-2 extents-overflow B-tree with 32 leaf records across three leaves.

evidence_fsck.extents_tree: depth=2 leafRecords=32 nodeSize=1024 blockSize=512 (parsed from the on-disk extents B-tree header node)
evidence_fsck.fsck_result: ** Checking extents overflow file. ... ** The volume SAKH4 appears to be OK.
evidence_fsck.delete_collapse: Deleting one fragmented file removes its 16 records from the depth-2 tree (leafRecords 32 -> 16); fsck_hfs stays clean - proves multi-leaf record removal + merge on a real volume.
evidence_fsck.note: fsck_hfs's 'Checking extents overflow file', 'Checking catalog file' and 'Checking volume bitmap' phases validate every inline and overflow extent of every file against the allocation bitmap and the catalog with zero defects.

evidence_unit: tests/unit/test_partition_manager_core.cpp: hfsFileSystemWriter_collapsesDepthTwoExtentsTreeOnDelete (depth-2 load + multi-leaf remove + merge + depth collapse) and hfsFileSystemWriter_splitsLeafBelowExistingIndexNode (leaf split below an existing index node -> three-leaf tree, root index has three children). Full local suite 140/140 green. clang-format / lizard (CCN<=10, params<=5) / cppcheck (exhaustive) all clean.
kernel_mount: CERTIFIED 2026-06-25 in a macOS Sequoia VM (qemu/KVM recovery, apfs/hfs kernel). The fragmented image (sha256 57EF87E46FE8CFB31E3DAE151B17AB7F3599AD65A112B9820B51D5495C2B4A31) was attached on SATA; the macOS kernel MOUNTED the bare unjournaled HFS+ volume ('Volume SAKH4 on SAKH4 mounted'); 'ls -l /Volumes/SAKH4' showed frag_a.bin + frag_b.bin both 67072 bytes; the kernel READ both files back BYTE-PERFECT across the depth-2 multi-leaf extents-overflow tree ('INTEG a=0 b=0' = every byte of each file is its fill char, so the kernel correctly reassembled ~131 single-block fragments per file from the inline 8 extents + multi-leaf overflow records). Then Apple's NATIVE fsck_hfs (version hfs-683.100.9) on /dev/rdisk0: '** Checking extents overflow file. ... ** The volume SAKH4 appears to be OK.' Evidence hfs.h4-kernel-mount-readback.png + hfs.h4-apple-fsck-PASS.png. The H4 lane flips to Yes.
